methodologies.name: Agentic Workflow Construction
methodologies.summary: Build an agent out of four named parts before you write any loop. The four parts are the model, the tools, the data stores, and the loop prompt that ties them together. Also pick how much freedom the model gets each turn. That choice is the interaction paradigm: passive, explicit, or autonomous. Keep every part separate so you can swap one without touching the rest. The more freedom you give the model, the more safety checks the loop needs.
methodologies.intent: Make agent authors name the four parts and the freedom level before they code, so a failure points to one part instead of smearing across a vague agent.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Use this when you build a new agent from scratch. It fits a coding agent, a customer-service agent, a browser agent, or an internal-tools agent. Do it on day one, before you write the loop. Don't apply it when you are just wiring up a single model call with no tools or memory. That is a prompt, not an agent.

inputs.name: Task specification
inputs.description: What the agent has to do, written as something a user can ask for.

inputs.name: Available model(s)
inputs.description: The models the agent can call. Note each one's cost, speed, and what it is good at.

inputs.name: Tool inventory
inputs.description: The outside services the agent may use: APIs, databases, search, browsers, sandboxes.

inputs.name: Data store(s)
inputs.description: The places the agent reads and writes: memory stores, vector indexes, caches, scratchpads.

outputs.name: Named four-component agent
outputs.description: An agent whose model, tools, data stores, and loop prompt are each built on their own and easy to swap.

outputs.name: Declared interaction paradigm
outputs.description: The chosen freedom level. Passive means the model only writes text. Explicit means the model uses tools when told to. Autonomous means the model picks its own tools and keeps going until the job is done.

outputs.name: Agent loop
outputs.description: The control flow that runs the four parts under the freedom level you picked.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  spec[Task specification] --> s1[Pick interaction paradigm]
  models[Available models] --> s2[Author model component]
  s1 --> s2
  tools[Tool inventory] --> s3[Author tool component]
  s2 --> s3
  stores[Data stores] --> s4[Author data-store component]
  s3 --> s4
  s4 --> s5[Author agent-loop prompt]
  s5 --> s6[Add guardrails and verifiers]
  s6 --> s7[Bind to orchestration framework if needed]
  s7 --> out1[Named four-component agent]
  s7 --> out2[Declared interaction paradigm]
  s7 --> out3[Agent loop]

methodologies.example_scenario:
A four-person team at a healthtech startup is building an intake-triage agent. It reads incoming clinical-message threads and either drafts a reply, escalates to a clinician, or files the thread. Inputs they had: a task spec from the clinical-ops lead, GPT-4.1 and Claude Sonnet as candidate models, a tool inventory consisting of an EHR lookup, a triage-rubric API, and an internal escalation endpoint, plus a Postgres scratchpad and a vector index over previous threads.

They followed the methodology in order. For the freedom level they chose explicit, not autonomous, because every escalation has regulatory implications. For the model they picked Claude Sonnet with a structured-output schema for the triage decision. For tools they wrote three typed functions with JSON schemas, each checked on return. For data stores they used a scratchpad for the current thread and a vector index for similar past cases. The loop prompt was 40 lines naming the tools, the stop condition, and the schema. For guardrails they added a PHI-redaction step on input and an allowlist on the escalation endpoint.

What they learned: they almost reached for LangGraph on day one. But the hand-rolled loop fit on one screen and made debugging the first three failures easy. They moved to LangGraph only in week three, when retries and observability became real needs. Because each part had a name, a tool-schema bug was found and fixed in 20 minutes. It did not turn into a vague 'the agent is weird' ticket.
methodologies.deep_dive:
The four components map to a clean separation of concerns. **Model**: the foundation model plus system-prompt skeleton and any structured-output schema — this is where capability lives. **Tools**: typed function signatures with model-facing descriptions; returns are validated against their JSON schema before re-entering the loop. **Data stores**: the memory shape (scratchpad, KV store, vector index, episodic log) with explicit read-on-step and write-after-step policies. **Agent-loop prompt**: the control flow binding the three together, with the tool list, stop condition, and success state made explicit.

The interaction paradigm is a latitude dial. **Passive** = pure generation, tools and memory invisible to the model. **Explicit** = the loop invokes tools at fixed points, the model only decides content. **Autonomous** = the model selects actions each step and continues until a stop condition fires. Guardrailing scales monotonically with the paradigm: passive needs almost none; autonomous needs input/output guardrails, tool-call validators, and a hard stop condition. Defer orchestration frameworks (LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, Pydantic AI) until the loop genuinely needs fan-out, retries, observability, or a state-machine shape — a hand-rolled loop is correct for the first cycle.

steps.name: Pick the interaction paradigm
steps.description: Pick how much freedom the model gets. Passive: it only writes text and cannot see tools or memory. Explicit: the loop calls tools at set points and the model just decides the content. Autonomous: the model decides what to do at each step. Pick the lowest level that solves the task. More freedom costs more and is risky to turn on too early.

steps.name: Author the model component
steps.description: Choose the model, the rough shape of the system prompt, and the output format if you need one. This is where the smarts live. Everything else is plumbing.

steps.name: Author the tool component
steps.description: List the tools as typed function signatures with the descriptions the model will see. Each tool returns structured data, not loose prose. Check every tool return against its schema before it goes back into the loop.

steps.name: Author the data-store component
steps.description: Pick the memory shape: scratchpad, key-value store, vector index, or event log. Decide what the model reads at each step. Decide what the loop writes after each step.

steps.name: Author the agent-loop prompt
steps.description: Write the loop prompt that ties the model, tools, and data stores together. Say clearly which tools exist, when the loop ends, and what success looks like.

steps.name: Add guardrails and verifiers
steps.description: For autonomous agents, add input and output guardrails, tool-call checks, and a stop condition. The more freedom the model has, the more checks the loop needs.

steps.name: Bind to an orchestration framework only when needed
steps.description: If the loop needs fan-out, retries, observability, or a state-machine shape, move it into a framework such as LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, or Pydantic AI. Do not reach for one on day one. A hand-rolled loop is fine for the first cycle.

principles: Name all four parts. Debugging an unnamed part is debugging fog.
Pick the lowest freedom level that solves the task.
Tool returns are structured data, never loose prose.
Safety checks grow with freedom. Passive needs almost none. Autonomous needs many.

known_failure_modes.note: Adding tools without a typed schema until the model can't reliably pick the right one — failures smear across components.
Stuffing the loop prompt with every conceivable instruction until the model loses focus.
Choosing the autonomous paradigm when explicit would have sufficed, then needing heavy guardrails to compensate.

sources.title: Designing Large Language Model Applications
sources.chapter: Ch 10 'Interfacing LLMs with External Tools' — Components of an Agentic System
sources.quote: Models, Tools, Data Stores, Agent Loop Prompt, Guardrails and Verifiers, Agent Orchestration Software

sources.title: AI Agents in Action
sources.chapter: Ch 1 'Introduction to agents and their world' (Manning liveBook preview); Ch 4 'Exploring multi-agent systems'
sources.quote: Defining the concept of agents ... Differentiating the components of an agent ... Analyzing the rise of the agent era: Why agents? ... Peeling back the AI interface ... Navigating the agent landscape

methodologies.name: SPAR Agent Loop Design
methodologies.summary: Build the agent's core as a loop with four named phases: Sense, Plan, Act, Reflect. Sense takes in signals from the environment and the task input. Plan works out what the agent intends to do next. Act runs the tool calls and makes the changes. Reflect checks the outcome against the plan and updates what the agent believes. Make each phase a real, named step you can point to in code and see in traces. Then you can tell which phase is failing instead of staring at a black box.
methodologies.intent: Give every agent the same four named phases, Sense, Plan, Act, and Reflect, so behaviour, traces, and failures line up with a phase instead of hiding in one murky loop.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Use this when you design the main loop of any real agent: a coding agent, a research agent, a browser agent, or an ops agent. It helps most when a team is reaching for ReAct or a free-running loop with no clear control structure. Don't apply it for a single-shot prompt or a fixed pipeline with no perception and no reflection. There is no loop to design.

inputs.name: Environment interface
inputs.description: The channels the agent reads from: APIs, user turns, the file system, and other observers.

inputs.name: Task goal
inputs.description: The goal the agent is chasing, written so the Plan phase can break it into steps.

inputs.name: Action repertoire
inputs.description: The tools, effectors, or sub-agents the Act phase can use.

outputs.name: Four-phase agent loop
outputs.description: Clear Sense, Plan, Act, and Reflect steps joined by typed transitions. You can test and watch each step on its own.

outputs.name: Phase trace schema
outputs.description: Structured trace events from each phase that your tooling can group, search, and replay.

diagram.mermaid:
stateDiagram-v2
  [*] --> Sense
  Sense --> Plan: observation ready
  Plan --> Act: plan emitted
  Act --> Reflect: outcome observed
  Reflect --> Sense: continue
  Reflect --> [*]: stop condition met
  Reflect --> Plan: replan needed
  note right of Act: only side-effects here

methodologies.example_scenario:
An infra team at a B2B SaaS company is rebuilding their on-call triage agent. The old version was one big ReAct prompt. It 'worked' but was impossible to trace when it misrouted an incident. They adopted SPAR. Inputs: a PagerDuty webhook stream as the environment, the goal 'route incident to the right responder with context', and an action set of runbook lookup, log query, Slack post, and PagerDuty re-assign.

Build order: the Sense phase pulls the alert payload, the last 15 minutes of related logs, and any open incidents on the affected service. The Plan phase produces a typed plan ('lookup runbook X, then post summary, then re-assign to team Y') using chain-of-thought. The Act phase is the only one that can post or re-assign. Sense and Plan stay read-only. The Reflect phase checks the route against a rule of thumb: was the responder on-call, did they ack within 5 minutes? It then updates a belief about runbook accuracy.

Results: traces now group cleanly by phase. When the agent misroutes, the team sees right away which phase failed. Two weeks in, they found Reflect was rubber-stamping bad routes because it shared the Plan prompt. They swapped in a different model for Reflect and the false-route rate dropped from 14% to 4%. The step budget caught a Plan-to-Reflect oscillation that would have looped forever in the old design.
methodologies.deep_dive:
SPAR (Sense-Plan-Act-Reflect) reifies the agent control loop as four addressable runtime stations joined by typed transitions, with a read/write contract: only **Act** may cause side-effects; **Sense**, **Plan**, and **Reflect** stay read-only. This isolation is what makes failures localise.

- **Sense** assembles the situation (user input, tool outputs, memory recall, environment state) without interpreting it; surface ambiguity rather than collapse it prematurely.
- **Plan** turns the sensed state into an intended trajectory — chain-of-thought, tree-of-thoughts, or explicit plan-and-execute, emitting a plan artefact rather than a free monologue.
- **Act** executes via tool calls, sub-agent dispatch, or effectors.
- **Reflect** compares outcome to plan intent and updates beliefs, decides loop/replan/escalate/stop.

**Termination** is enforced with a step budget and composable stop conditions, bounding Plan↔Reflect oscillation. Two recurring failure modes: *same-model self-critique* (Reflect sharing Plan's model and prompt rubber-stamps decisions — fix by using a distinct model/prompt for Reflect) and *hidden-state coupling* (phases sharing mutable state outside their typed transitions, which destroys the localisation guarantee). Each phase emits phase-tagged trace events so observability tooling can group, filter, and replay by station.

steps.name: Build the Sense phase
steps.description: Decide what the agent observes each tick: user input, tool outputs, memory recall, and environment state. Sense gathers the situation, it does not judge it. Surface anything unclear instead of papering over it.

steps.name: Build the Plan phase
steps.description: Turn the sensed situation into an intended next move or plan. Use chain-of-thought, tree-of-thoughts, or plan-and-execute when the task calls for it. The output is a plan, not a free monologue.

steps.name: Build the Act phase
steps.description: Run the plan through tool calls, sub-agents, or outside effectors. Act is the only phase that may change anything. Sense, Plan, and Reflect stay read-only.

steps.name: Build the Reflect phase
steps.description: Check the outcome of Act against what the plan intended. Update beliefs, note lessons, and decide whether to loop, replan, escalate, or stop. Reflect is what makes the agent more than a script.

steps.name: Wire phase transitions and termination
steps.description: Define the typed moves between phases: Sense to Plan to Act to Reflect, then back to Sense or stop. Add a step budget and clear stop conditions so the loop always ends.

steps.name: Instrument each phase as a first-class trace event
steps.description: Emit a structured trace event tagged with its phase. Your observability tools can then group, filter, and replay by phase, so failures stay easy to find.

principles: Every agent has the same four phases. Make them visible, do not hide them.
Only Act changes things. The other three observe, decide, and learn.
Reflect is required. An agent that never reflects is just a script.
Phase boundaries are contracts you can test, not nice-sounding labels.

known_failure_modes.note: Loop runs Sense→Plan→Act→Reflect→Sense forever because Reflect lacks a stop condition or step budget.
Reflect uses the same model and prompt as Plan, so the agent rubber-stamps its own decisions.
Phases share mutable state outside their typed transitions, breaking the localisation guarantee SPAR is supposed to give.

sources.title: Agentic Artificial Intelligence: Harnessing AI Agents to Reinvent Business, Work, and Life
sources.chapter: Ch 4 'Inside the Mind of an AI Agent' (pp. 83–107)
sources.quote: Inside The Mind Of An AI Agent (pages 83-107)

sources.title: How to see the bigger picture re: AI agents
sources.quote: what makes AI agents powerful is how these four capabilities work together in an integrated cycle, creating a system that can pursue complex goals with increasing sophistication

methodologies.name: BDI Agent Construction Methodology
methodologies.summary: Build an agent from three parts that map to how people act on purpose. The beliefs are what the agent holds true. The desires are the goals it may take on. The intentions are the goals it has committed to and is acting on. A reasoning cycle drives it: each round it picks a plan from a plan library to meet a goal. This is the belief-desire-intention (BDI) approach from the Jason and JaCaMo line of tools. Beliefs are first-class data. Plans are first-class code. The cycle is out in the open. It shines when an LLM-only agent cannot show its goals or explain its reasoning, which BDI does by default.
methodologies.intent: Make agents whose inner state, the beliefs, desires, and intentions, is written down and easy to inspect, so the behaviour can be explained and checked instead of just emerging.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Reach for BDI when an agent must reason openly about goals, stick with long-running commitments, coordinate with other agents under shared rules, or stand up to an audit by a regulator. It fits multi-agent setups on tools such as Jason, JaCaMo, or Jadex. Don't apply it for one-shot generation, a light tool-using chatbot, or any task where no goal lives past a single turn. The BDI machinery is overhead for stateless work. Skip it too when the team has no appetite for symbolic agent programming.

inputs.name: Domain ontology
inputs.description: The set of facts the agent can believe: facts about the world, about other agents, and about itself.

inputs.name: Goal catalogue
inputs.description: The goals the agent may take on, each with a priority and the conditions that trigger it.

inputs.name: Plan library
inputs.description: The agent's how-to knowledge: plans filed by the event and context that triggers them.

outputs.name: BDI agent program
outputs.description: An agent definition, for example in AgentSpeak or Jason, with its beliefs, goals, and plan library.

outputs.name: Deliberation cycle configuration
outputs.description: The rules for picking which event, plan, and intention to act on, plus the settings for the reasoning cycle.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  ont[Domain ontology] --> s1[Model belief base]
  goals[Goal catalogue] --> s2[Enumerate desires and adoption conditions]
  plib[Plan library input] --> s3[Author plan library]
  s1 --> s2
  s2 --> s3
  s3 --> s4[Define selection functions]
  s4 --> s5[Wire deliberation cycle]
  s5 --> s6[Integrate with environment and organisation]
  s6 --> out1[BDI agent program]
  s6 --> out2[Deliberation cycle config]
  s5 -.-> cycle[Perceive -> Update beliefs -> Select event -> Choose plan -> Execute intention]

methodologies.example_scenario:
A research group at a logistics firm is prototyping a multi-agent yard-management system on JaCaMo. Each truck-dispatch agent must reason about goals such as unload before dock close, prioritise refrigerated cargo, and avoid blocking lanes. It must commit to those goals over minutes to hours and coordinate with other agents under a shared organisation. They tried a pure LLM agent first and dropped it, because regulators required an auditable goal-trace.

Inputs: a domain ontology of yard facts (dock_open, cargo_type, lane_blocked), a goal catalogue with priorities, and a plan library drawn from dispatcher SOPs. The team modelled the beliefs with perceptual facts refreshed every two seconds and persistent facts updated on each event. They split the goals into achievement goals (unload truck T) and maintenance goals (keep aisle 3 clear). They wrote the plan library as small triggering-event plus context plus body units. They overrode the selection rules so refrigerated cargo always wins ties. The reasoning cycle ran at 0.5 Hz. Finally they attached the agents to CArtAgO artefacts for docks and lanes, and a MOISE organisation defined the dispatcher and mover roles.

What they learned: because BDI agents commit to a goal, they did not drop an unload mid-task when a higher-priority goal arrived. They queued the new goal as a fresh intention instead. The auditors accepted the BDI trace as proof of why each decision was made. An LLM ReAct trace had failed that test.
methodologies.deep_dive:
This operationalises the Jason/JaCaMo lineage of agent-oriented programming (AgentSpeak). The architecture:

- **Belief base**: predicates over the world, other agents, and the self, partitioned into *perceptual* beliefs (refreshed each reasoning cycle from percepts) and *persistent* beliefs (updated on events).
- **Desires/goals**: distinguished as *achievement goals* (bring about a state, e.g. `unload(T)`) versus *maintenance goals* (keep a condition true, e.g. `aisle_clear(3)`), each with triggering conditions and priorities.
- **Plan library**: plans of the form *triggering-event : context <- body*, where the body sequences belief updates, sub-goals, and external actions. Keep plans small and composable.
- **Selection functions**: the event-, plan-, and intention-selection functions (S_E, S_O, S_I) that resolve ties; Jason/JaCaMo ship defaults but non-trivial domains should override them.
- **Deliberation cycle**: perceive → update beliefs → select event → choose applicable plan → execute intention, run at a domain-appropriate rate.

The defining property is **commitment semantics**: an adopted intention persists until it is achieved, dropped, or shown impossible, rather than being abandoned the moment a higher-priority desire appears. On JaCaMo, the environment is programmed as CArtAgO artefacts and the social layer as a MOISE organisation (roles, groups, schemes). Watch for *infinite-debate* (plan selection oscillating between two equally applicable plans without committing) and *hidden-state coupling* (plans mutating shared beliefs as side-effects, which breaks the explicit BDI contract).

steps.name: Model the belief base
steps.description: Decide the facts the agent holds about the world, about itself, and about other agents. Split them into perceptual facts, refreshed each cycle, and persistent facts that stay until something changes them.

steps.name: Enumerate desires and adoption conditions
steps.description: List the goals the agent may pursue. Give each one a trigger and a priority. Tell achievement goals (reach a state) apart from maintenance goals (keep a state true).

steps.name: Author the plan library
steps.description: Write each plan as a trigger, a context, and a body. The body is a list of belief updates, sub-goals, and outside actions. Plans are the unit of how-to knowledge. Keep them small and easy to combine.

steps.name: Define selection functions
steps.description: Say how the agent picks among events, plans, and intentions when more than one applies. Jason and JaCaMo give you defaults, but override them for any real domain.

steps.name: Wire the deliberation cycle
steps.description: Set up the perceive-decide-act cycle: read the environment, update beliefs, pick an event, choose a plan, run the intention. Tune the cycle rate to fit the domain.

steps.name: Integrate with environment and organisation
steps.description: On JaCaMo, attach the agent to an environment (CArtAgO artefacts) and a social organisation (MOISE roles, groups, and schemes). This is where multi-agent coordination is grounded.

principles: Beliefs, desires, and intentions are real artefacts you can read, inspect, and debug.
Plans come from a library, not from improvising. Combine plans instead of hoping for emergence.
Commitment matters. An intention stays until it is met, dropped, or proven impossible.
Program the environment and the organisation alongside the agent. Do not assume them.

known_failure_modes.note: Plan-selection logic loops between two equally applicable plans without committing — the agent deliberates forever.
Plans mutate shared beliefs as side-effects, breaking the explicit BDI contract.

sources.title: Multiagent Systems (2nd edition)
sources.quote: 13 Programming Multiagent Systems Rafael H. Bordini and Jürgen Dix

sources.title: Multi-Agent Oriented Programming: Programming Multi-Agent Systems Using JaCaMo
sources.chapter: Book-length treatment of the same JaCaMo lineage (Boissier, Bordini, Hübner, Ricci)
sources.quote: Multi-Agent Oriented Programming: Programming Multi-Agent Systems Using JaCaMo

methodologies.name: Behavior Tree Back-Chaining Construction
methodologies.summary: Build an agentic behavior tree by working backwards from the goal. Start at the goal. Add an action whose result meets the goal. That action has its own needs, so add actions that meet those needs. Keep going until every leaf is something you can run directly. Behavior trees give you tidy, modular control flow. Working backwards (back-chaining) means every node is there for a reason, not bolted on by hand. Leaves mix LLM calls and tool calls. The tree itself becomes the record of how the agent's behaviour was put together.
methodologies.intent: Build a behavior tree where every node exists only because it helps meet its parent's needs, so there are no dead branches and no hand-bolted structure.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Use this when you need structured agent control flow that mixes LLM nodes and tool nodes: game-like worlds, multi-step task agents, or involved workflows. It helps most when a prompt-only agent has turned into spaghetti. Don't apply it for a simple straight-line pipeline or a single-shot prompt, where a tree is just overhead. Skip it too when the task is truly open-ended exploration that you cannot break into goals.

inputs.name: Goal specification
inputs.description: The end state the agent must reach, written as a condition you can check true or false.

inputs.name: Action inventory
inputs.description: The candidate actions: LLM prompts, tool calls, and sub-agents. Each one lists what it needs before it runs and what it makes true after.

outputs.name: Agentic behavior tree
outputs.description: A runnable tree. The root is the goal. The leaves are real LLM or tool actions. Every node is there because back-chaining put it there.

outputs.name: Pre/post-condition map
outputs.description: For each node, what must be true before it runs and what it makes true after. This is the contract the tree was built against.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  goal[Goal specification] --> s1[State goal as root precondition]
  inv[Action inventory] --> s2[Find actions whose post-conditions satisfy parent]
  s1 --> s2
  s2 --> s3[Recurse on each child precondition]
  s3 --> dec{Leaf reached?}
  dec -- no --> s2
  dec -- yes --> s4[Choose composite node type]
  s4 --> s5[Wire leaves to LLM and tool calls]
  s5 --> s6[Validate tree end-to-end]
  s6 --> out1[Agentic behavior tree]
  s6 --> out2[Pre/post-condition map]

methodologies.example_scenario:
A games-studio R&D team is building a quest-companion NPC agent. The old prompt-only version drifted off-quest because the LLM had no built-in way to track what was still needed. They moved to an Agentic Behavior Tree built by back-chaining. Inputs: the goal 'player completes the rescue quest' as a checkable condition, and an action inventory of LLM dialogue prompts, world-state tool calls (open_door, give_item, teleport_npc), and a sub-agent that handles combat.

Starting from the goal, they looked for actions whose result made 'rescue_complete' true. Only give_item(prisoner, freedom_token) did. That action needs the prisoner to be reachable and the token in inventory, so each became a sub-goal. Going deeper, reachability broke into open_door plus path_clear, and inventory broke into find_token plus pickup. They used sequence nodes for sub-goals that all have to happen, and a fallback node for two alternative routes to the prisoner. They wired the leaves to ReAct prompts for dialogue and to engine tool calls for changing the world.

To check the tree they walked it from the leaves up. They found one branch that assumed 'door_open' but no child ever made it true, so they added a missing unlock-door action. Result: the companion stopped wandering. Every node was there because the tree needed it. The pre/post-condition map became the audit trail the QA team used for quest regression.
methodologies.deep_dive:
Back-chaining is goal-regression planning realised as a behavior tree. Begin with the goal as the root precondition, then for each unmet precondition search the action inventory for an action whose **post-conditions** entail it; that action becomes a child, and its own preconditions become new sub-goals. Recursion terminates when a precondition is satisfied by the initial state or is directly observable.

- **Composite node selection is semantic, not stylistic**: use a *sequence* when children's effects are conjunctive (all must hold), a *fallback/selector* when they are alternative routes, and *parallel* when they are independent. Leaves return success/failure/running so the standard BT tick semantics compose correctly.
- **Leaves are heterogeneous**: ReAct-style LLM calls, deterministic tool invocations, or sub-agent dispatches — agentic behavior trees are explicitly not LLM-only.
- **Validation is leaves-up**: verify that the combined post-conditions of a node's children entail that node's precondition; trim dead branches and repair gaps (the classic gap is an assumed condition no child actually asserts, e.g. `door_open`).

Known failure modes: *premature closure* (treating a partially-true post-condition as fully satisfied and skipping a needed branch) and *tool-explosion* (each leaf bolting on a new tool without consolidation, so the leaf layer degrades even while the tree above stays clean). The pre/post-condition map doubles as the audit artefact.

steps.name: State the goal as a root precondition
steps.description: Write the agent's end goal as a condition to satisfy. That condition becomes the precondition of the root node.

steps.name: Find actions whose post-conditions satisfy the parent
steps.description: Search the action inventory for actions whose result makes the parent's condition true. Each one you find becomes a child node.

steps.name: Recurse on each child's preconditions
steps.description: Treat each child's needs as new sub-goals and repeat. Stop when an action's needs are already true at the start or are easy to observe.

steps.name: Choose composite node types
steps.description: For each non-leaf node, pick how its children combine. Use a sequence when all must happen, a fallback when they are alternatives, and parallel when they are independent.

steps.name: Wire leaves to LLM calls and tool invocations
steps.description: Build each leaf as a ReAct-style LLM call, a tool call, or a sub-agent. Each leaf returns success, failure, or running, so the tree behaves correctly.

steps.name: Validate the tree end-to-end
steps.description: Walk the tree from the leaves up. Check that each node's children together make its condition true. Trim or fix any branch that does not.

principles: Every node exists because it meets its parent's need. No bolt-on nodes.
Before and after conditions are real contracts, not comments.
Pick the composite node on purpose: sequence for all-of, fallback for one-of, parallel for independent.
Leaves mix LLM and tool calls. Behavior trees are not LLM-only.

known_failure_modes.note: Tree treats a partially-true post-condition as fully satisfied and skips a needed branch.
Each leaf adds a new tool without consolidation, so the leaf layer becomes unmanageable even though the tree above it is clean.

sources.title: AI Agents in Action
sources.chapter: Ch 6 'Building autonomous assistants', §6.5 'Building ABTs with back chaining'

sources.title: "AI Agents in Action" book: A Deep Dive into the Future of Intelligent Systems
sources.quote: Agentic Behavior Trees (ABTs) sequence a series of nodes that consist of LLM calls and tool use

methodologies.name: Four-Tier Agent Memory Construction
methodologies.summary: Build agent memory as four parts that work together. Conversational memory holds the recent turns. Semantic memory holds facts and their embeddings. Episodic memory holds traces of past interactions. Procedural memory holds learned skills and routines. Each part has its own rules for what to write, how to look things up, and how to shrink it when it gets too big. This rejects the shortcut of using one vector store for everything. It makes the team decide, for each part, what is stored, how it is retrieved, and how it is compressed when it fills up.
methodologies.intent: Replace 'agent memory is one vector store' with four clear parts, conversational, semantic, episodic, and procedural, each with its own rules.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Use this when you design memory for any agent that must remember things beyond a single conversation: assistants, coding agents, and long-running ops agents. It helps most when a team has hit recall problems with one plain vector store. Don't apply it for single-turn agents and stateless tools. Skip it too when the model's context window already covers the memory needs and you do not need lookup at all.

inputs.name: Memory budget
inputs.description: The limits for each part: how many tokens, how much storage, and how much delay you can afford.

inputs.name: Recall scenarios
inputs.description: The questions or moments where the agent must remember something, and which part should answer each one.

outputs.name: Four-tier memory architecture
outputs.description: Conversational, semantic, episodic, and procedural stores, each with a clear interface.

outputs.name: Per-tier policies
outputs.description: Write, lookup, and compression rules for each part, written down and easy to tune.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  budget[Memory budget] --> s1[Build conversational tier]
  recall[Recall scenarios] --> s1
  s1 --> s2[Build semantic tier]
  s2 --> s3[Build episodic tier]
  s3 --> s4[Build procedural tier]
  s4 --> s5[Define retrieval orchestration across tiers]
  s5 --> s6[Define compression and forgetting policies]
  s6 --> out1[Four-tier memory architecture]
  s6 --> out2[Per-tier policies]
  s1 -.-> conv[(Conversational)]
  s2 -.-> sem[(Semantic)]
  s3 -.-> epi[(Episodic)]
  s4 -.-> proc[(Procedural)]

methodologies.example_scenario:
A team building a long-running personal-finance assistant saw users complain that it 'forgot' simple facts after a few weeks. At the same time it kept dragging in context that did not matter. They had used a single pgvector index for everything. They rebuilt memory as the four parts. Inputs: a memory budget of 8k tokens per turn and about 1GB of storage per user, plus recall scenarios pulled from real user logs (such as 'what's my emergency-fund target?', 'how did we handle my Q2 bonus last year?', and 'do my usual end-of-month sweep').

Conversational part: a sliding window that keeps the last six turns plus a rolling summary. Semantic part: extracted facts such as target_emergency_fund=$15000, stored in a vector index for similarity lookup. Episodic part: per-interaction transcripts, timestamped and tagged, searchable by date and topic. Procedural part: 'end-of-month sweep' stored as a routine with steps the agent could replay. Lookup routing: fact questions hit semantic first, 'last time' questions hit episodic, recurring-task questions hit procedural. Compression: episodic memories shrank to summaries after 90 days, and a routine was promoted only after three successful runs.

The forgetting rule was the surprise win. They chose to drop stale facts, such as the payroll cadence from the user's old job, instead of letting them spoil lookups. Recall-precision on the eval set went from 0.62 to 0.89. The 'agent dragged in random old context' complaints stopped.
methodologies.deep_dive:
Each tier is a distinct subsystem with its own write policy, retrieval mechanism, and compression strategy — the methodology explicitly rejects the single-vector-store collapse.

- **Conversational** (short-term): the live turn window. Truncation policy is a design choice — sliding window, summary-augmented truncation, or hierarchical summarisation — plus a promotion rule for what graduates to longer-lived tiers.
- **Semantic**: extracted facts stored as embeddings, keyed for similarity (dense) retrieval. Requires an extraction policy deciding what is fact-shaped versus what stays episodic.
- **Episodic**: timestamped, identified interaction transcripts — the agent's autobiography — queryable by recency and topic for 'what happened last time' recall.
- **Procedural**: replayable skills/routines/successful plans, typically promotion-gated (e.g. only after N successful executions).

**Retrieval orchestration** routes a recall request to the right tier(s) and merges/prioritises results, rather than delegating to one index. **Compression and forgetting** is per-tier: summarisation, clustering, recency-weighted decay, or forced eviction; deliberate forgetting is a precision lever, not just a capacity control. Crisp tier boundaries also defend against three failures: *memory-poisoning* (unauthenticated promotion from conversational into semantic/procedural), *context-fragmentation* (disconnected fragments recombining incoherently when orchestration is missing), and *memo-as-source-confusion* (the agent's own notes later retrieved as if they were source evidence).

steps.name: Build the conversational tier
steps.description: Keep a window on the live conversation. Pick how you trim it (sliding window, summary plus window, or layered summaries) and decide what gets promoted to the longer-lived parts.

steps.name: Build the semantic tier
steps.description: Store extracted facts and their embeddings so you can look them up by similarity. Decide what counts as a fact and what stays as an episode.

steps.name: Build the episodic tier
steps.description: Save real past interactions and events with timestamps and IDs. Episodes are the agent's diary. You use them to recall what happened last time.

steps.name: Build the procedural tier
steps.description: Store skills, routines, and plans that worked, so the agent can replay them. This is how the agent gets faster and better at a recurring task.

steps.name: Define retrieval orchestration across tiers
steps.description: Decide which part answers which kind of recall request. Decide how to merge or rank results when more than one part replies.

steps.name: Define compression and forgetting policies
steps.description: Give each part a way to shrink: summarise, cluster, decay by recency, or drop on purpose. Without these rules the parts grow without limit.

principles: Memory is not one store. It is four parts that work together with different jobs.
Every part has its own write, read, and compression rules.
Look-ups are routed across the parts, not handed to a single vector index.
Forgetting is a feature. Design it on purpose.

known_failure_modes.note: Without per-tier write policies, attacker-controlled content can be promoted from conversational to semantic or procedural memory.
Tiers retrieve disconnected fragments that recombine into an incoherent context — symptom of missing retrieval orchestration.
Notes the agent wrote to itself in conversational memory are later retrieved as if they were source evidence — a confusion that crisp tier boundaries prevent.

sources.title: AI Agents in Action
sources.chapter: Ch 8 'Understanding agent memory and knowledge', §8.6 'Implementing memory in agentic systems', §8.7 'Understanding memory and knowledge compression'
sources.quote: 8.6 Implementing memory in agentic systems ... 8.7 Understanding memory and knowledge compression

sources.title: "AI Agents in Action" book: A Deep Dive into the Future of Intelligent Systems
sources.quote: implementing memory in agentic systems, and memory/knowledge compression

methodologies.name: Plan-Reason-Evaluate-Feedback Loop
methodologies.summary: Build the agent's control logic as a loop with four stages. Plan drafts a candidate approach. Reason fills it in using chain-of-thought or tree-of-thoughts. Evaluate scores the result, using self-consistency or a judge. Feedback hands the lessons back to Plan for the next round. Make each stage its own step with its own metrics, so the team can tune them one at a time. The thing to avoid is one giant prompt trying to do all four jobs and doing each one badly.
methodologies.intent: Split the agent's control loop into Plan, Reason, Evaluate, and Feedback so each one can be written, tested, and tuned on its own instead of crammed into a single prompt.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Use this when the task is hard enough to need real planning and self-checking: research agents, coding agents, and multi-step problem solvers. It helps most when one big ReAct prompt has stopped improving. Don't apply it for single-shot generators and simple tool callers, where four stages on a trivial task is overkill. Skip it too when your latency budget cannot absorb the extra round-trips.

inputs.name: Task specification
inputs.description: What the agent must do, in a form you can break into sub-steps.

inputs.name: Evaluator definition
inputs.description: The judge, rubric, or self-consistency check that will score what Reason produces.

outputs.name: Four-stage control loop
outputs.description: Plan, Reason, Evaluate, and Feedback as four separate stages, each with its own metrics.

outputs.name: Stage-level metrics
outputs.description: A success or failure signal for each stage that your telemetry can graph and alert on.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  task[Task specification] --> plan[Plan stage]
  evalDef[Evaluator definition] --> eval[Evaluate stage]
  plan --> reason[Reason stage]
  reason --> eval
  eval --> fb[Feedback stage]
  fb -- accept --> done[Stop / emit result]
  fb -- replan --> plan
  fb -- reject with reasons --> reason
  plan -.-> tel[Per-stage metrics]
  reason -.-> tel
  eval -.-> tel
  fb -.-> tel
  budget[Step budget] --> fb

methodologies.example_scenario:
A platform team is building an internal research-assistant agent. It produces market-sizing memos for the strategy department. Their first big ReAct prompt stalled at memo-quality 6/10 on the strategy team's rubric. They split the control loop into PREF stages. Inputs: a task spec ('produce a market-sizing memo for product X in region Y') and an evaluator: a 12-criterion rubric scored by a separate Claude model with the strategy team's example memos in context.

The Plan stage emits two candidate approaches using multi-path plan generation. The Reason stage fills in each one with tree-of-thoughts, including reasoning about source quality. The Evaluate stage scores both against the rubric, running it three times for self-consistency. The Feedback stage either accepts the winner, rejects it with reasons tied to specific rubric criteria (such as 'sizing assumption unsupported'), or asks Plan to try a different angle. The iteration budget is three loops.

What they learned: most of the gain came from making Evaluate independent. The same-model self-critique they started with always rated the memo at 8/10, no matter the quality. Switching to a different model with the rubric in context lowered the average rated score but raised the real quality, as judged by humans, from 6/10 to 8.4/10. The per-stage metrics showed that Reason was the slowest stage, but Plan was where the biggest quality jumps came from. That sent their tuning effort to the right place.
methodologies.deep_dive:
PREF decomposes the agent control loop into four independently instrumented stations so each is separately authorable, testable, and tunable.

- **Plan**: single-path or multi-path plan/trajectory generation, chosen by cost/risk profile.
- **Reason**: generative elaboration via chain-of-thought or tree-of-thoughts — do *not* collapse this into Plan, since it is where trajectory detail and source-quality reasoning live.
- **Evaluate**: scores the reasoning output via self-consistency, an LLM-as-judge, or a rubric. The non-negotiable property is **evaluator independence** — it must be able to dissent. *Same-model self-critique* (Evaluate sharing Reason's model and context) systematically rubber-stamps; the fix is a distinct model and/or rubric-in-context, which often *lowers* the rated score while *raising* human-judged quality.
- **Feedback**: converts the evaluation into an actionable signal — accept, reject-with-criterion-specific-reasons, replan, or escalate — and closes the loop (Reflexion-style).

**Termination** requires a max-iterations budget plus a convergence test, guarding against *infinite-debate* (Feedback reopening Plan without convergence) and *unbounded-loop* (no stage holding the authority to declare done). Per-stage telemetry is mandatory: shared telemetry makes independent tuning impossible, and it is what reveals where quality leverage actually sits (often Plan, not the slowest stage).

steps.name: Author the Plan stage
steps.description: Draft a candidate plan or approach. Use single-path or multi-path plan generation depending on cost and risk.

steps.name: Author the Reason stage
steps.description: Fill in the plan with chain-of-thought or tree-of-thoughts. Reasoning generates new detail. Do not fold it back into Plan.

steps.name: Author the Evaluate stage
steps.description: Score what Reason produced. Use self-consistency, an LLM judge, or a rubric. Evaluate must be independent enough to disagree.

steps.name: Author the Feedback stage
steps.description: Turn the score into a clear next step for the planner: accept, reject with reasons, replan, or escalate. Feedback is what closes the loop.

steps.name: Instrument each stage independently
steps.description: Emit traces and metrics scoped to each stage. You cannot tune the four stages if they share one set of telemetry.

steps.name: Bound iterations
steps.description: Add a max-iterations budget and a test for when to stop. Otherwise the loop can bounce between plan and reflection forever.

principles: Plan, Reason, Evaluate, and Feedback are four jobs. Give each its own prompt and metric.
The evaluator must be able to disagree. Same-model self-critique is a failure mode, not a method.
Feedback closes the loop, or it is not feedback.
Bound the iterations. Set a budget and a test for when to stop.

known_failure_modes.note: Evaluate uses the same model and context as Reason and rubber-stamps every result.
Feedback keeps reopening the plan without convergence; missing iteration budget or convergence test.
Feedback returns to Plan unboundedly because no stage has authority to declare done.

sources.title: AI Agents in Action
sources.chapter: Ch 11 'Agent planning and feedback', §11.5 'Applying planning, reasoning, evaluation, and feedback to assistant and agentic systems'
sources.quote: 11.5 Applying planning, reasoning, evaluation, and feedback to assistant and agentic systems ... 11.5.1 Application of assistant/agentic planning ... 11.5.2 Application of assistant/agentic reasoning ... 11.5.3 Application of evaluation to agentic systems ... 11.5.4 Application of feedback to agentic/assistant applications

sources.title: The Four Pillars of Effective AI Agents: Planning, Reasoning, Evaluation, and Feedback
sources.quote: planning, reasoning, evaluation, and feedback ... tackle complex challenges, learn from experiences, and continuously improve

methodologies.name: Agent Count Escalation
methodologies.summary: Start with one agent. Add a second only when the first clearly fails the task and you can show it. When you do add agents, pick the coordination style on purpose: democratic, manager, hierarchical, or actor-critic. Do not reach for a swarm by reflex. Treat the number of agents as a dial you turn only when the evidence says to. Pair each step up with a coordination shape that fits the failure you saw.
methodologies.intent: Make 'how many agents' a decision driven by evidence, and force a deliberate choice of coordination style at each step up, instead of jumping to multi-agent by reflex.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Use this when a team wants to design a multi-agent system before a single agent even exists, or when one agent has stopped improving and the next move is unclear. Don't apply it when the task is multi-actor by nature, such as negotiation, simulation, or debate as a feature. There the multi-agent shape is part of the requirement. Skip it too when you have no measurement of how the single agent fails, because then you have nothing to base a step up on.

inputs.name: Single-agent baseline metrics
inputs.description: Numbers that show where the single agent succeeds and where it fails.

inputs.name: Coordination cost budget
inputs.description: The latency, tokens, and infra you can spend on adding more agents.

outputs.name: Agent-count decision
outputs.description: A written choice of one, a few, or many agents, with the evidence that backs it.

outputs.name: Coordination shape
outputs.description: The named coordination style (democratic, manager, hierarchical, actor-critic, or swarm) with clear roles.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  base[Single-agent baseline metrics] --> s1[Build and measure single-agent baseline]
  budget[Coordination cost budget] --> s3
  s1 --> dec{Single agent meets bar?}
  dec -- yes --> ship1[Ship single agent]
  dec -- no --> s2[Identify specific failure escalation should fix]
  s2 --> s3[Pick coordination style]
  s3 --> s4[Add smallest number of agents to resolve failure]
  s4 --> s5[Re-measure against same eval set]
  s5 --> dec2{Gains material?}
  dec2 -- yes --> s6[Document escalation as decision record]
  dec2 -- no --> rb[Roll back to baseline]
  s6 --> out1[Agent-count decision]
  s6 --> out2[Coordination shape]

methodologies.example_scenario:
A small ML platform team at a fintech was pushed by leadership to 'go multi-agent' for their compliance-document-review agent. They held out long enough to apply the methodology. Inputs: a single-agent baseline already in production, hitting 78% F1 on the compliance-eval set with median latency 12s per document, and a coordination cost budget of +$0.05 per document and +20s latency. The failures clustered in two places. First, the single agent missed cross-reference mismatches between sections. Second, it sometimes produced overly cautious findings.

They named the failure first: the evaluator was not independent, because the same agent both reads and judges, so it talks itself out of the mismatches. That points to actor-critic, not a swarm. They added one critic agent with a different system prompt and a different model. They kept the original agent as the actor and re-measured. F1 rose to 0.87 and latency to 18s, both within budget. They wrote the step up as a decision record. It named the conditions for climbing further, such as cross-jurisdictional documents that need a specialist critic.

A junior PM had proposed a five-agent democratic council for the same problem. The methodology produced a two-agent system that hit the quality bar at a fraction of the coordination cost. The decision record later stopped a re-escalation impulse when a new failure appeared. The team revisited it, saw the new failure was not on the recorded triggers, and fixed it inside the existing actor-critic shape instead.
methodologies.deep_dive:
Agent count is treated as an evidence-gated tuning dial, not an architectural starting point. Each escalation is justified by a *named* failure mode and matched to a coordination topology that addresses it:

- **Democratic**: peers generate and vote/aggregate — fits diversity-of-perspective and reducing single-model bias.
- **Manager (orchestrator-workers)**: one orchestrator decomposes and dispatches to workers — fits task decomposition and parallelism; watch for *orchestrator-as-bottleneck*.
- **Hierarchical**: nested supervisors over sub-teams — fits deep decomposition with span-of-control limits.
- **Actor-critic (evaluator-optimizer)**: one agent generates, a distinct one judges — fits the *evaluator-independence* failure where a single agent that both produces and self-judges rationalises away its own errors (use a different model/prompt for the critic).

The discipline: build and measure a single-agent baseline on a representative eval set; add the *minimum* agents that resolve the named failure (two is frequently sufficient); re-measure on the *same* metrics and roll back if gains are marginal; record the escalation as a decision record with explicit de-escalation conditions. Failure modes to guard: *hero-agent* (refusing to split an overloaded agent), *unbounded-subagent-spawn* (escalating to 'as many as needed' instead of a bounded topology), and *multi-agent-sequential-degradation* (information loss at each handoff when no coordination style was deliberately chosen).

steps.name: Build and measure the single-agent baseline
steps.description: Build the simplest single agent that could plausibly solve the task. Measure its success, latency, and failures on a representative eval set.

steps.name: Identify the specific failure that escalation should fix
steps.description: Name the failure the second agent will fix: context overflow, role conflict, a missing specialist skill, or a judge that is not independent. Without a named failure, adding agents is just theatre.

steps.name: Pick the coordination style
steps.description: Choose on purpose. Democratic: peers vote. Manager: one orchestrator with workers. Hierarchical: supervisors nested over sub-teams. Actor-critic: one agent generates, another judges. Each one fits a different failure.

steps.name: Add the smallest number of agents that resolves the failure
steps.description: Two is often enough. Do not leap to a swarm. Extra agents add coordination cost and bring new failures of their own.

steps.name: Re-measure against the same eval set
steps.description: Compare the multi-agent system to the single-agent baseline on the same metrics. If the gains are small, roll back.

steps.name: Document the escalation as a decision record
steps.description: Write down why you stepped up, what failure it fixed, and when stepping back down would be right. The next team needs this.

principles: Single agent first. Multi-agent is a tuning move, not a starting point.
Every step up is paired with a named failure it fixes.
Coordination style is a deliberate pick from a short list, not the framework default.
Re-measure after stepping up. Roll back if the gains are small.

known_failure_modes.note: Single agent is overloaded with responsibilities the team refuses to split, even after the failure mode is named — escalation needed but not taken.
Escalation went to 'as many agents as needed' instead of a bounded coordination shape — coordination cost explodes.
Chained agents lose information at each handoff because no coordination style was deliberately chosen.
Manager-style coordination was picked without checking whether the orchestrator becomes the new single point of failure.

sources.title: Building Applications with AI Agents
sources.quote: How Many Agents Do I Need? ... Swarms and principles for agent escalation ... Multiagent coordination methods (democratic, manager-based, hierarchical, actor-critic)

sources.title: Choosing the Right Multi-Agent Architecture
sources.quote: Start with a single agent and good prompt engineering. Add tools before adding agents. Graduate to multi-agent patterns only when you hit clear limits.

methodologies.name: MAESTRO Threat Modeling
methodologies.summary: Run an agent-specific security review before you ship, using the MAESTRO categories. They are: risks from the model itself, threats to the data, attacks from inside and outside the system, and red-team probing. This takes classic threat modeling, such as STRIDE and LINDDUN, and adapts it to how agents get attacked. That includes prompt injection, memory poisoning, tool misuse, data theft, and agent-on-agent attacks. The output is a list of threats, each one tied to a concrete defence.
methodologies.intent: Replace a generic security review with an agent-aware one that lists the attack types specific to agents and pairs each with a concrete defence before you ship.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Run this before any agent goes to production, above all when it has tool access, a free-running loop, multi-agent coordination, or sensitive data. Run it again after every real change in capability. Do not use it instead of normal application security. MAESTRO adds to AppSec, it does not replace it. Don't apply it when the system really is a single-turn chat with no tools, no memory, and no access to private data. There is no agent attack surface to model.

inputs.name: Agent architecture
inputs.description: The model, tools, memory, loop, and integrations. Together they make up the attack surface.

inputs.name: Data classification
inputs.description: What data the agent reads, writes, or sends, and how sensitive each piece is.

inputs.name: Adversary model
inputs.description: Who would attack this agent and what they want: an outside attacker, a malicious user, a compromised tool, or a hostile agent.

outputs.name: Threat catalogue
outputs.description: A list of threats, grouped by MAESTRO category, each with a severity and a likelihood.

outputs.name: Defensive control map
outputs.description: For each high-priority threat, the specific defence you put in place: a pattern, a guardrail, or an isolation boundary.

outputs.name: Red-team report
outputs.description: What you found when you attacked the agent yourself against the listed threats.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  arch[Agent architecture] --> s1[Enumerate foundation-model threats]
  cls[Data classification] --> s2[Enumerate data-protection threats]
  adv[Adversary model] --> s3[Enumerate internal threats]
  arch --> s4[Enumerate external threat vectors]
  s1 --> s5[Map defensive controls to each threat]
  s2 --> s5
  s3 --> s5
  s4 --> s5
  s5 --> s6[Red-team agent against catalogue]
  s6 --> out1[Threat catalogue]
  s6 --> out2[Defensive control map]
  s6 --> out3[Red-team report]

methodologies.example_scenario:
A SaaS company is getting ready to ship its first agent feature. It is an autonomous email-triage assistant that reads customer support inboxes, drafts replies, and can update Zendesk tickets. Security agreed to ship only after a MAESTRO pass. Inputs: the four-component agent architecture (a GPT-4 model; three tools, gmail.read, gmail.search, and zendesk.update; pgvector memory of past tickets; an autonomous loop), a data classification noting that 12% of tickets contain PII and 0.5% contain payment data, and an adversary model covering outside attackers who craft malicious customer emails, malicious vendors in the supply chain, and curious internal staff.

Model threats: prompt injection through crafted ticket bodies, and the model leaking its system prompt. They handled these with input filtering and a dual-LLM split. Data threats: PII leaving the model and landing in Zendesk drafts. They paired this with output filtering and a redaction step. Internal threats: memory poisoning, where a malicious customer plants false 'preference' facts that bias future replies. They handled this with signed write provenance on the memory store and a human in the loop on any reply that cites a memory entry younger than 24h. External threats: a supply-chain compromise of the embedding provider. They pinned the vendor and added a fallback. Red-team probe: they sent 40 adversarial emails through a sandboxed instance. 3 managed to extract hidden data, and all were closed before launch.

The team learned that 'we have AppSec' was not enough. The memory-poisoning class of threats simply did not exist on their earlier systems and needed brand-new controls.
methodologies.deep_dive:
MAESTRO (Multi-Agent Environment, Security, Threat, Risk, & Outcome) adapts classical threat modeling (STRIDE, LINDDUN) to the agentic attack surface. The four enumeration axes and their characteristic threats:

- **Foundation-model threats**: jailbreaks, prompt injection (direct and indirect via tool/retrieval content), system-prompt exfiltration, hallucination, sycophancy, training-data extraction. Controls: input/output guardrails, prompt-injection defenses, dual-LLM separation.
- **Data-protection threats**: PII/secrets/proprietary content crossing the model or tool boundary, traced across context, memory, tool calls, and outputs. Controls: redaction, secrets handling, output filtering.
- **Internal threats**: memory poisoning, agent scheming, hidden mode switching, role drift, agent-on-agent attacks. Controls: sandbox/subagent isolation, signed write provenance, quorum/authentication on memory writes.
- **External threats**: supply-chain compromise of tools/models/embedding providers, hostile tool outputs, malicious documents in retrieval corpora, network-level attacks. Controls: vendor pinning, fallback providers, treating tool output as untrusted.

Every catalogued threat is bound to a concrete control (input/output filtering, sandbox, dual-LLM, allowlist, human-in-the-loop, policy gate) or an explicit accepted-risk note. **Red-teaming is in-scope, not a follow-up**: adversarial prompts, malicious tool outputs, and crafted memory injections, run in a sandboxed reproduction. Re-run the whole pass after every material capability change. Documented failure modes: a memory threat catalogued but not paired with authentication/quorum (*memory-poisoning*), a tool-access threat modelled without a policy gate (*agent-privilege-escalation*), and tool outputs trusted verbatim because no control was tied to that threat.

steps.name: Enumerate foundation-model threats
steps.description: List the risks that come from the model itself: jailbreaks, prompt injection, hallucination, sycophancy, and pulling data out of training. Decide which ones you must defend against.

steps.name: Enumerate data-protection threats
steps.description: Find where sensitive data enters, sits in, or leaves the agent. Track PII, secrets, and proprietary content across context, memory, tool calls, and outputs.

steps.name: Enumerate internal threats
steps.description: List threats from inside the agent system: memory poisoning, agent scheming, hidden mode switching, role drift, and agent-on-agent attacks.

steps.name: Enumerate external threat vectors
steps.description: List threats from outside: a compromised tool or model in the supply chain, hostile tool outputs, malicious documents in a retrieval corpus, and network attacks.

steps.name: Map defensive controls to each threat
steps.description: For each high-priority threat, name the defence: input filtering, output filtering, a sandbox, a dual-LLM split, an allowlist, a human in the loop, or a policy gate.

steps.name: Red-team the agent against the catalogue
steps.description: Attack the live agent with hostile prompts, malicious tool outputs, and planted memory entries. Write down where the defences held and where they broke.

principles: Threat modeling for agents is not generic AppSec. It must list agent-specific threats.
Every listed threat is paired with a concrete defence or a written note that you accept the risk.
Red-team probing is part of the method, not an optional follow-up.
Re-run the pass after every real change in capability: new tools, new data, more freedom.

known_failure_modes.note: Memory threat was acknowledged in the catalogue but not paired with an authentication or quorum control.
Tool-access threat was modelled but no policy-gating control was added before deployment.
External-threat enumeration missed the tool-provider supply chain.
Tool outputs treated as trustworthy because no defensive control was tied to that specific threat.

sources.title: Building Applications with AI Agents
sources.quote: Emerging threat vectors specific to agentic systems ... Red Teaming and threat modeling with MAESTRO ... Data privacy, encryption, and provenance ... Internal and external safeguards

sources.title: Agentic AI Threat Modeling Framework: MAESTRO
sources.quote: MAESTRO (Multi-Agent Environment, Security, Threat, Risk, & Outcome) ... a novel threat modeling framework designed specifically for the unique challenges of Agentic AI

methodologies.name: Agent Architecture Decision Ladder
methodologies.summary: Pick the agent architecture from a four-step ladder. The steps are: a plain model call, a single agent, a fixed multi-agent workflow, and an autonomous multi-agent system. Pick the lowest step that solves the task. This forces a choice you can defend, instead of jumping straight to an autonomous multi-agent system out of habit. Treat architecture as a cost-versus-benefit call, with clear criteria at each step.
methodologies.intent: Make the architecture choice a deliberate climb up a four-step ladder, backed by evidence, picking the lowest step that solves the task, instead of defaulting to an autonomous multi-agent system.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Use this at project kickoff, before any architecture is built, and again at every big capability change that might push you up a step. Don't apply it when the architecture is fixed for you, such as a vendor mandate or an existing framework that already locks the step. Skip it too when the team has no habit of measurement. Without evals to compare steps, the ladder is just opinion.

inputs.name: Task profile
inputs.description: What kind of task it is: text generation, taking actions, structured teamwork, or open-ended exploration.

inputs.name: Determinism requirement
inputs.description: How much variation the stakeholders will accept in the agent's behaviour and output.

inputs.name: Cost and latency budget
inputs.description: The limits per task: tokens, infra spend, and total response time.

outputs.name: Architecture decision
outputs.description: The chosen step, with the reasoning written down. It is one of: plain model, single agent, fixed multi-agent workflow, or autonomous multi-agent system.

outputs.name: Escalation triggers
outputs.description: The named conditions that would justify climbing to the next step later.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  prof[Task profile] --> s1[Profile task on ladder dimensions]
  det[Determinism requirement] --> s1
  cost[Cost and latency budget] --> s1
  s1 --> r1{Tools or memory needed?}
  r1 -- no --> rung1[Step 1: plain model call]
  r1 -- yes --> r2{Decomposable into fixed graph?}
  r2 -- no --> rung2[Step 2: single agent]
  r2 -- yes --> r3{Determinism required?}
  r3 -- yes --> rung3[Step 3: deterministic MAS workflow]
  r3 -- no --> rung4[Step 4: autonomous MAS]
  rung1 --> doc[Document decision and escalation triggers]
  rung2 --> doc
  rung3 --> doc
  rung4 --> doc
  doc --> out1[Architecture decision]
  doc --> out2[Escalation triggers]

methodologies.example_scenario:
An enterprise data team is scoping three internal AI features at once: an FAQ chatbot for HR policies, a procurement agent that drafts and submits purchase orders, and an R&D 'paper-of-the-week' summariser that crawls arXiv. A junior architect proposed an autonomous multi-agent system for all three. The lead applied the decision ladder.

FAQ chatbot: it is text generation, with no tools, deterministic answers wanted, and a tight latency budget. That is Step 1, a plain model call with a RAG wrapper. The team almost added an agent loop for 'feel', but the ladder said no. Procurement: it needs SAP tool access, a vendor-lookup tool, memory of approved vendors, and multi-step planning. That is Step 2, a single agent with the four-component architecture. R&D summariser: it breaks into a fixed pipeline (crawl, filter, cluster, summarise, rank), needs no emergent coordination, and wants deterministic results so editors can reproduce them. That is Step 3, a deterministic orchestrator-workers pipeline. None of the three needed Step 4.

They recorded the escalation triggers. The FAQ bot would climb to Step 2 only if HR needed it to file tickets. The procurement agent would climb to Step 3 only if specialist sub-agents (legal, finance) became necessary. The R&D pipeline would climb to Step 4 only if cross-domain synthesis needed agents to negotiate. Eight months later only the procurement use case actually climbed, and it climbed on evidence, not enthusiasm.
methodologies.deep_dive:
The ladder embodies a least-powerful-architecture rule, treating each step as a capability/cost step with explicit selection criteria:

- **Step 1 — plain model call**: text generation, no tools, no memory, no multi-step planning. Wrap in an API (optionally a RAG wrapper) and ship. Cost: cheapest, fully deterministic-ish.
- **Step 2 — single agent**: tool use, memory, or a loop is required. Build the four-component agent (model/tools/data-stores/loop). Most production agents stop here.
- **Step 3 — deterministic multi-agent workflow**: the task decomposes into a fixed graph of specialist steps. Use orchestrator-workers, pipes-and-filters, or prompt-chaining — *structure lives in the graph, not in the agents*, preserving determinism and reproducibility.
- **Step 4 — autonomous multi-agent system**: genuinely open-ended tasks needing emergent coordination or agent-to-agent negotiation (hierarchical agents, swarm, debate). You deliberately pay nondeterminism and coordination overhead.

The decision is profiled along four dimensions — action-taking need, decomposability, determinism requirement, and exploration-vs-exploitation — and recorded with named escalation triggers so future climbs are evidence-bound. Anti-patterns this guards against: *hero-agent* (stuck on Step 2 with one over-stuffed agent when the task is decomposable), *unbounded-subagent-spawn* (jumping to Step 4 without triggers), *demo-to-production-cliff* (picking the step that demoed well rather than the one production needs), and *top-tier-model-for-everything* (treating 'always go higher' as the default the way teams over-pick the biggest model).

steps.name: Profile the task on the ladder dimensions
steps.description: Rate the task on four things: does it need to take actions, does it break into parts, how deterministic must it be, and is it more about exploring or exploiting? These ratings decide the step.

steps.name: Step 1 — plain model call
steps.description: If the task is text generation with no tools, no memory, and no multi-step planning, stop at the model. Wrap it in an API and ship.

steps.name: Step 2 — single agent
steps.description: If the task needs tools, memory, or a loop, climb to a single agent with the four-component architecture. Most production agents stop here.

steps.name: Step 3 — deterministic multi-agent workflow
steps.description: If the task breaks into a fixed graph of specialist steps, build a deterministic multi-agent workflow with orchestrator-workers or pipes-and-filters. The structure lives in the graph, not in the agents.

steps.name: Step 4 — autonomous multi-agent system
steps.description: Climb here only when the task is truly open-ended, needs emergent coordination, or gains from agents negotiating with each other. Accept the cost of nondeterminism and coordination on purpose.

steps.name: Document the decision and escalation triggers
steps.description: Write down which step you chose and why. Name the conditions that would justify climbing higher later, so the next move rests on evidence, not vibes.

principles: Pick the lowest step that solves the task.
The architecture choice is a written decision backed by evidence, not a reflex.
Each step has a defining capability and a defining cost. Know both before you climb.
Name the escalation triggers up front. Climb on evidence, not enthusiasm.

known_failure_modes.note: Stuck on Step 2 with one over-stuffed agent because the team won't climb to Step 3 even when the task is decomposable.
Jumped to Step 4 without escalation triggers, ending up with an autonomous MAS for a task that Step 3 would have solved deterministically.
Picked the step that demoed well rather than the step the production task needs.
Treated step choice as 'always go higher' the same way teams treat model choice as 'always pick the biggest'.
